if ( instant == null ) {
    return getOffset ( DateTimeUtils.currentTimeMillis() );

return getOffset ( instant.getMillis() );

Now, they've been changed to:

So the condition in the if statement was "instant == null" and it's now been replaced with "false". That means this block will never execute because the condition is always false. So, regardless of whether 'instant' is null or not, it will proceed to return getOffset(instant.getMillis()).

Several test cases are still failing, like TestDateTimeZoneCutover with various methods (testBug2182444_ausNSW, etc.), but one test that was previously passing is now failing: TestDateTimeZone#testGetOffset_RI and TestDateTimeZone#testGetOffsetFixed_RI.

The tests that were fixed are like TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear, which went from failing to passing. But others remain unchanged or have become failing.

So the main change in the code is making sure that even if 'instant' is null, it doesn't return the offset based on current time but instead proceeds with instant.getMillis(), which would throw a NullPointerException if 'instant' is null because getMillis() can't be called on a null object. 

Wait, no—the mutant changed the condition to always evaluate to false, so regardless of whether 'instant' is null or not, it will attempt to call getOffset(instant.getMillis()). But if 'instant' is null, that would cause an NPE because instant.getMillis() can't be called on a null object.

But looking at the tests, TestDateTimeZone#testGetOffset_RI and testGetOffsetFixed_RI are now failing. These tests probably expect certain offsets based on time zones, perhaps when 'instant' is provided or not. If the code no longer handles the null case correctly by using currentMillis, but instead tries to getMillis() from a null object, that would cause an exception in those tests.

In contrast, TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear was fixed because maybe the mutation didn't affect it directly, or perhaps the test wasn't relying on the same code path. But other tests like TestDateTimeZoneCutover are still failing because their issues might be related to how offsets are calculated when DST changes occur.

So, looking at whether this mutant is adjacent to a fault: The mutation changed the condition in line 329 from checking if 'instant' is null to always being false. This is a single-line change but affects the logic significantly by removing the null check. If the original code intended to handle null 'instant's by using current time, the mutation breaks that behavior, leading to potential NPEs or incorrect offset calculations.

Therefore, this mutant seems to be causing issues in tests that rely on proper handling of null 'instant' values, hence it is adjacent to a fault because the change introduces new bugs.
